title: CIL Industrial Engineering — Full-length Mock 02
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: As per Income Tax Department data (June 2026), India's net direct tax collections in early FY27 rose by about what percentage year-on-year?
questions.solution: Net direct tax collections rose ~14.6% YoY to about Rs 5.21 trillion.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, which country became the first Central Asian member of the New Development Bank (NDB)?
questions.solution: Uzbekistan became the first Central Asian nation to join the BRICS-backed NDB.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which India-born theoretical physicist won the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics?
questions.solution: Rajasthan-born physicist Jainendra K. Jain received the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution ranked first in the QS World University Rankings 2027 (released June 2026)?
questions.solution: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) ranked first in QS World University Rankings 2027.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In June 2026, which Indian jurist was elected a Judge of the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)?
questions.solution: Indian jurist Bimal N. Patel was elected to ITLOS in June 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, PM Narendra Modi received the highest national award of which country?
questions.solution: PM Modi was conferred Slovakia's highest national award in 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026, in which an Indian Army contingent participated, was held in which country?
questions.solution: The multinational Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026 was held in Mongolia.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was named the new Chairman of GIC Re in June 2026?
questions.solution: The government named Hitesh Joshi as the new Chairman of GIC Re.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, ONGC was assigned to develop a new strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) facility at which location?
questions.solution: ONGC was tasked with building a new underground crude oil SPR cavern at Mangaluru, Karnataka.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The India–UK CETA and Social Security Agreement were reported to take effect from which date?
questions.solution: Reports indicated the India–UK CETA would take effect from July 15, 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was the first President of India?
questions.solution: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India (1950–62).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: How many Fundamental Rights are currently guaranteed by the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution: There are six Fundamental Rights after the Right to Property was removed in 1978.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Directive Principles of State Policy are enshrined in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.solution: Directive Principles are contained in Part IV (Articles 36–51).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who is the constitutional head of the Indian Union?
questions.solution: The President of India is the constitutional head of the Union.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national animal of India?
questions.solution: The Royal Bengal Tiger is the national animal of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national bird of India?
questions.solution: The Indian Peafowl (peacock) is the national bird of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national flower of India?
questions.solution: The lotus is the national flower of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the longest river in India?
questions.solution: The Ganga is the longest river flowing within India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the highest mountain peak entirely within India?
questions.solution: Kangchenjunga is the highest peak located within India's borders.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Tropic of Cancer passes through how many Indian states?
questions.solution: The Tropic of Cancer passes through eight Indian states.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the currency of Japan?
questions.solution: The Yen is the currency of Japan; Yuan is Chinese, Won is Korean.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the currency of the United Kingdom?
questions.solution: The UK's currency is the Pound Sterling (£).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Where are the headquarters of the United Nations located?
questions.solution: The UN headquarters is located in New York City, USA.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Where is the headquarters of the World Health Organization (WHO)?
questions.solution: The WHO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 5000 for 12 months and B invests Rs 6000 for 6 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 3000, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 5000×12 mo; B: Rs 6000×6 mo; profit Rs 3000.
**Working.** Ratio $=60000:36000$; share$_A=3000\times\tfrac{60000}{96000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 1875.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A vessel holds 40 L of milk. 10 L is drawn out and replaced with water; this is repeated 2 times. The milk remaining is about:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each draw-and-replace keeps the fraction $(1-x/V)$ of milk; after n repeats this fraction is raised to the n-th power.
**Formula.** $M=V\left(1-\tfrac{x}{V}\right)^{n}$
**Given.** V = 40 L, x = 10 L, n = 2.
**Working.** $=40\left(1-\tfrac{10}{40}\right)^{2}$.
**Answer.** $M\approx 22.5$ L.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The difference between compound and simple interest on Rs 10000 at 10% per annum for 2 years is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Over 2 years the gap between compound and simple interest is the first year's interest-on-interest.
**Formula.** $CI-SI=P\left(\tfrac{r}{100}\right)^2$
**Given.** P = Rs 10000, r = 10% p.a., t = 2 yr.
**Working.** $=10000\left(\tfrac{10}{100}\right)^2$.
**Answer.** $CI-SI=$ Rs 100.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A trader marks goods 50% above cost, then gives a 10% discount. His net profit percentage is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net selling factor is the markup factor times the discount factor; profit% is that factor reduced by 1.
**Formula.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{m}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{d}{100}\right)$
**Given.** Markup m = 50%, discount d = 10%.
**Working.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{50}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{10}{100}\right)=1.35$.
**Answer.** Profit $=(f-1)\times100=35\%$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A pipe fills a tank in 5 h, but with a leak it takes 7 h. The leak alone can empty the full tank in:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The leak's rate is the fill-rate without the leak minus the slower fill-rate with the leak.
**Formula.** $T=\tfrac{ab}{b-a}$
**Given.** Fill without leak a = 5 h; with leak b = 7 h.
**Working.** Leak rate $=\tfrac1{5}-\tfrac1{7}$; emptying time $=\tfrac{5\times7}{7-5}$.
**Answer.** $T=17.5$ h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Two trains 180 m and 130 m long move in opposite directions at 72 km/h and 45 km/h. The time to cross each other is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Opposite directions add speeds; convert km/h to m/s with the factor 5/18, then divide the combined length by the relative speed.
**Formula.** $t=\tfrac{L_1+L_2}{(s_1+s_2)\times\frac{5}{18}}$
**Given.** L₁ = 180 m, L₂ = 130 m, s₁ = 72 km/h, s₂ = 45 km/h.
**Working.** Relative $=(72+45)\times\tfrac{5}{18}=32.5$ m/s; $t=\tfrac{180+130}{32.5}$.
**Answer.** $t=9.54$ s.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An item bought for Rs 1200 is sold for Rs 1380. The profit percentage is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 8 men complete a work in 15 days, 6 men will complete it in:
questions.solution: Men-days constant: 8×15 = 6×D, D = 20 days.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A car covers a distance at 45 km/h in 4 hours. The distance is:
questions.solution: Distance = speed × time = 45 × 4 = 180 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: What is the average of the first 12 natural numbers?
questions.solution: Average of first n naturals = (n+1)/2 = (12+1)/2 = 6.5.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The HCF of 36 and 48 is:
questions.solution: The highest common factor of 36 and 48 is 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The LCM of 14 and 21 is:
questions.solution: The lowest common multiple of 14 and 21 is 42.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A man goes at 30 km/h and returns over the same route at 60 km/h. His average speed is:
questions.solution: Average speed = 2uv/(u+v) = 2×30×60/(30+60) = 40 km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A and B invest in the ratio 4 : 7. Out of a total profit of Rs 13200, A's share is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Pipe A fills a tank in 6 h and pipe B in 3 h. Together they fill it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: What is 12% of 520?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Simple interest on Rs 6000 at 10% per annum for 4 years is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An article sold at Rs 480 incurs a 20% loss. Its cost price is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The square root of 4225 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The percentage increase when a value rises from 40 to 50 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: 3 kg of rice at Rs 20/kg is mixed with 3 kg at Rs 50/kg. The average price per kg is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A sum doubles itself in 20 years under simple interest. The annual rate is:
questions.solution: To double, SI = P, so rate = 100/T = 100/20 = 5%.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: If 42 is divided in the ratio 2 : 5, the larger part is:
questions.solution: Larger part = 42 × 5/7 = 30.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A 150 m long train crosses a pole in 10 seconds. Its speed is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A can do a work in 15 days and B in 20 days. Working together they finish it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 5 km towards north, then turns east and walks 12 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(5² + 12²) = √169 = 13 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: X is the husband of Y. Z is the mother of Y. How is Z related to X?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Mother-in-law.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 5, 11, 23, 47, 95, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 95 × 2 + 1 = 191.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'FISH' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: FISH → HKUJ.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A = 1, B = 2, … , Z = 26, what is the sum of the letter values of 'BID'?
questions.solution: Add the positional values of B, I, D to get 15.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 1 January is a Friday, what day is 8 January?
questions.solution: 8 − 1 = 7 days, exactly one week, so it is again Friday.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row of 20 people, X is 9th from the left. X's position from the right is:
questions.solution: Position from right = 20 − 9 + 1 = 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A person facing north turns 90° to the right. Now they face:
questions.solution: Turning right from north leads to East.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: W, U, S, Q, ?
questions.solution: Letters decrease by 2 each time, so the next letter is O.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Hand : Glove :: Foot : ?
questions.solution: Just as Glove relates to Hand, Sock relates to Foot.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: M, N, O, P, ?
questions.solution: Consecutive alphabet letters; after P comes Q.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: 64, 125, 200, 216?
questions.solution: The others are perfect cubes; 200 is not.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.solution: Each number is halved, so 12 → 6.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 1, 4, 9, 16, ?
questions.solution: These are perfect squares; next = 5² = 25.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: The angle between the hands of a clock at 4:00 is:
questions.solution: Each hour = 30°; at 4:00 the hands are 4 × 30 = 120° apart.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which of these fractions is the largest?
questions.solution: Comparing decimal values, 4/5 is the largest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Pointing to a man, a woman said, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
questions.solution: The only daughter of her mother is the woman herself, so she is the man's mother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A=1, B=2, ... then the sum of the letters of ACE is:
questions.solution: Add the alphabet positions of A, C, E to get 9.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: Copper, Iron, Oxygen, Zinc?
questions.solution: Oxygen belongs to a different category from the rest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: All pens are tools. All tools are useful. Which conclusion follows?
questions.solution: Both premises are universal affirmatives, so the chained conclusion 'All pens are useful' follows.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 5, 7, 11, 17, ?
questions.solution: Differences grow by 2; adding the next difference gives 25.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 6 km north, then 8 km east. His distance from the start is:
questions.solution: Resultant = √(6² + 8²) = 10 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: GH, JK, MN, ?
questions.solution: Each pair skips one letter; after MN comes PQ.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 3, 9, 27, 81, ?
questions.solution: Each term is multiplied by 3, so next = 81 × 3 = 243.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: 3 : 9 :: 5 : ? (pattern n : n^2)

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a speech made without preparation':
questions.solution: An unprepared speech is delivered 'extempore'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: A place where birds are kept is called:
questions.solution: An 'aviary' houses birds; an apiary keeps bees.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to break the ice' means:
questions.solution: 'Break the ice' means to initiate conversation in a tense or new setting.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'a piece of cake' means:
questions.solution: 'A piece of cake' means something very easy to do.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'once in a blue moon' means:
questions.solution: 'Once in a blue moon' means something happening very rarely.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to bury the hatchet' means:
questions.solution: 'Bury the hatchet' means to end a quarrel and make peace.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to let the cat out of the bag' means:
questions.solution: It means to accidentally reveal a secret.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Child' is:
questions.solution: 'Child' has the irregular plural 'children'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Mouse' is:
questions.solution: 'Mouse' has the irregular plural 'mice'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Neither he nor I ___ wrong.'
questions.solution: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the nearer subject 'I', so 'am'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Each of the students ___ a book.'
questions.solution: 'Each' is singular, so it takes 'has'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is ___ honest man.'
questions.solution: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound, so 'an' is used.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'She plays ___ violin beautifully.'
questions.solution: Musical instruments take the definite article 'the'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'John wrote the letter.' is:
options: The letter is written by John
The letter was written by John
questions.solution: Past simple active becomes 'was written' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'She is reading a book.' is:
options: A book is being read by her
A book has been read by her
A book was read by her
A book is read by her
questions.solution: Present continuous active becomes 'is being read' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'He / don't / like / coffee.'
questions.solution: Third-person singular needs 'doesn't', not 'don't'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'One of the boys / are / playing / outside.'
questions.solution: 'One of the boys' is singular, so it requires 'is'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'I ___ my homework already.'
questions.solution: 'Already' signals present perfect, so 'have done' is correct.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'By next year, she ___ here for a decade.'
questions.solution: A completed action by a future point uses the future perfect 'will have worked'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the word that means the opposite of 'Artificial'.
questions.solution: 'Artificial' is opposite to 'natural'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct meaning of the word 'Frugal'.
questions.solution: 'Frugal' means careful with money, i.e. economical.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Abundant'.
questions.solution: 'Abundant' means existing in large quantity, i.e. plentiful.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Brief'.
questions.solution: 'Brief' means short or concise.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Happy'.
questions.solution: 'Happy' is synonymous with 'joyful'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Enormous'.
questions.solution: 'Enormous' means very large, i.e. huge.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The control limits on a Shewhart control chart are usually set at:
options: ±3 standard deviations from the centre line
questions.solution: 3σ limits give ~99.7% of points inside when the process is in control (low false alarms).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: ABC analysis classifies inventory items by their:
questions.solution: 'A' items have high value and tight control; 'C' items are low value with loose control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Component A (R = 0.9) is in series with a parallel pair B (0.85) and C (0.9). The system reliability is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In CPM/PERT, the critical path is the:
questions.solution: The critical path's length sets the minimum project duration; its activities have zero float.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A process has USL = 112, LSL = 88, mean = 100, σ = 2. Its Cpk is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The process capability index Cpk, unlike Cp, accounts for:
options: Process centring relative to the specification limits
questions.solution: Cpk penalizes off-centre processes; Cp assumes perfect centring and uses only the spread.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A transportation problem seeks to minimize cost of shipping from sources to destinations subject to:
questions.solution: It balances supplies at origins against demands at destinations at least total cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The last three demands were 120, 110 and 140 units. The 3-period moving-average forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Hungarian method is an efficient algorithm for solving the:
questions.solution: The Hungarian algorithm optimally assigns n jobs to n workers (one-to-one) at minimum cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With demand 4000/yr, order cost ₹50 and order quantity 200, the annual ordering cost is:
questions.solution: Annual ordering cost = (D/Q)×S = (4000/200)×50 = ₹1000.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The break-even point is the output level at which:
questions.solution: At break-even, contribution covers fixed cost exactly, so profit is zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a process (functional) layout, similar machines are grouped:
questions.solution: Process layout suits low-volume, high-variety (job-shop) production.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Annual demand = 5000 units, ordering cost = ₹100/order, holding cost = ₹4/unit/yr. The EOQ is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The variance of a PERT activity time is:
questions.solution: Activity variance σ² = ((tp − to)/6)²; path variance is the sum along the critical path.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A PERT activity has to = 4 and tp = 16 days. Its standard deviation is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The simplex method is used to solve:
questions.solution: Simplex moves along the vertices of the feasible polytope to the optimum of an LP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Time study is used to establish the:
questions.solution: Time study (stopwatch) determines how long a qualified worker should take at a defined pace.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Annual demand = 1000 units, ordering cost = ₹100/order, holding cost = ₹8/unit/yr. The EOQ is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every linear programming problem (the primal) has an associated:
options: Dual problem with related optimal value
questions.solution: By LP duality, the primal and dual optima are equal (strong duality) when both are feasible.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Customers arrive at 20/hour and spend on average 0.5 hour in the system. The average number in the system is:
questions.solution: Little's law: L = λ·W = 20×0.5 = 10.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In single exponential smoothing, the new forecast is:
questions.solution: Ft+1 = α·At + (1−α)·Ft; it is a weighted average favouring recent data as α rises.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an LP, the function being optimized (maximized or minimized) is the:
questions.solution: The objective function expresses the goal (e.g. profit, cost) as a linear combination of decision variables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two components of reliability 0.95 and 0.9 are in series. The system reliability is:
questions.solution: Series: Rs = R₁×R₂ = 0.95×0.9 = 0.86.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The feasible region of a linear program is always:
questions.solution: Intersection of half-spaces is convex, so the LP optimum lies at a vertex of the convex feasible region.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 60, UCL = 72, LCL = 48), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
In control with only chance variation
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 72) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Little's law relates the average number in a system (L), arrival rate (λ) and time in system (W) as:
questions.solution: Little's law: average inventory/queue L = arrival rate × average time in system.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The balance (line) efficiency of an assembly line is:
options: Sum of task times ÷ (number of stations × cycle time)
questions.solution: Efficiency = Σti/(N·C); the complement is balance delay (idle fraction).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With α = 0.3, last actual = 250 and last forecast = 280, the next exponential-smoothing forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Productivity is defined as the ratio of:
questions.solution: Productivity = output produced ÷ resources (input) consumed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With demand 4000/yr, order cost ₹40 and order quantity 200, the annual ordering cost is:
questions.solution: Annual ordering cost = (D/Q)×S = (4000/200)×40 = ₹800.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A good material-handling system aims to:
options: Move material efficiently while minimizing handling
questions.solution: The principle is least handling: move the right material efficiently and safely.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: At the Economic Order Quantity, the annual ordering cost equals the:
questions.solution: EOQ is where the rising holding-cost curve crosses the falling ordering-cost curve — the two are equal.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the inventory cost curve shown (D = 2000, S = ₹40, H = ₹5), the order size Q* that minimizes total cost (where ordering and holding costs are equal) is:
questions.solution: Total cost is minimum at the EOQ Q* = √(2DS/H) = √(2×2000×40/5) ≈ 178.9 units, where the two cost curves cross.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 140" width="260" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M40 25 C 90 90 110 100 240 112" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="60" y="40" font-size="8" fill="#16a34a">ordering</text><line x1="35" y1="118" x2="235" y2="40" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="190" y="55" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">holding</text><path d="M40 60 C 110 30 120 30 235 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="120" y="28" font-size="8" fill="#2563eb">total cost</text><line x1="120" y1="48" x2="120" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><text x="110" y="134" font-size="8" fill="#475569">Q*</text></svg>
figure.caption: Inventory total-cost curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Pareto chart is used in quality control to:
options: Identify the vital few causes of most defects
questions.solution: The Pareto (80/20) chart ranks defect causes so effort targets the most significant few.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With α = 0.3, last actual = 200 and last forecast = 180, the next exponential-smoothing forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Activities on the critical path have a total float of:
questions.solution: Any delay on a zero-float (critical) activity delays the whole project.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two components each with reliability 0.9 in parallel give a system reliability of:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 4+6, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 3+10), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 10, 1-3-4 = 13. The critical path is the longest = 13 days.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: PERT differs from CPM mainly in that PERT treats activity times as:
questions.solution: PERT uses optimistic, most-likely and pessimistic estimates; CPM uses single deterministic times.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two components of reliability 0.8 and 0.85 are in parallel. The system reliability is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In acceptance sampling, a Type I error (producer's risk) means:
questions.solution: Type I (α): a genuinely acceptable lot is rejected; Type II (β): a bad lot is accepted.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Depreciation in engineering economics represents:
options: The decline in value of an asset over time
questions.solution: Depreciation allocates an asset's cost over its useful life as it loses value.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two components of reliability 0.8 and 0.7 are in parallel. The system reliability is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Ergonomics (human factors) is mainly concerned with:
options: Fitting the job/workplace to the worker
Fitting the worker to the machine only
questions.solution: Ergonomics designs tasks and workplaces around human capabilities to improve safety and efficiency.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 5+6, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 2+5), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 11, 1-3-4 = 7. The critical path is the longest = 11 days.

figure.caption: CPM activity-on-arrow network

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The reorder point (under constant demand) is given by:
options: Demand rate × lead time (+ safety stock)
questions.solution: Reorder point = demand during lead time, plus safety stock if demand/lead time vary.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The break-even quantity equals fixed cost divided by the:
options: Contribution margin per unit (price − variable cost)
questions.solution: Q_BE = FC/(p − v); each unit's contribution covers fixed cost until break-even.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A worker's observed time is 5 min at a performance rating of 120%. The normal time is:
questions.solution: Normal time = observed × rating = 5×120% = 6 min.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Standard time is obtained from normal time by adding:
questions.solution: Standard time = normal time × (1 + allowance fraction).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 3+8, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 2+5), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 11, 1-3-4 = 7. The critical path is the longest = 11 days.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 140" width="280" height="140"><circle cx="30" cy="70" r="14" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="30" y="74" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">1</text><circle cx="140" cy="30" r="14" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="140" y="34" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">2</text><circle cx="140" cy="110" r="14" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="140" y="114" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">3</text><circle cx="250" cy="70" r="14" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="250" y="74" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">4</text><line x1="42" y1="62" x2="128" y2="38" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="80" y="42" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">A=3</text><line x1="152" y1="36" x2="238" y2="62" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="200" y="42" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">B=8</text><line x1="42" y1="78" x2="128" y2="104" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="80" y="108" font-size="9" fill="#16a34a">C=2</text><line x1="152" y1="104" x2="238" y2="78" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="200" y="108" font-size="9" fill="#16a34a">D=5</text></svg>
figure.caption: CPM activity-on-arrow network

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Gantt chart primarily displays:
options: Activity schedules against a time axis
questions.solution: A Gantt (bar) chart shows each task's start/finish along a timeline.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Six Sigma process targets a defect level of about:
questions.solution: Six Sigma (with 1.5σ shift) corresponds to ~3.4 DPMO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 50, UCL = 62, LCL = 38), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: In control with only chance variation
Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 62) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 130" width="280" height="130"><line x1="20" y1="30" x2="260" y2="30" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="262" y="33" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">UCL</text><line x1="20" y1="70" x2="260" y2="70" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="262" y="73" font-size="8" fill="#16a34a">CL</text><line x1="20" y1="110" x2="260" y2="110" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="262" y="113" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">LCL</text><polyline points="40,68 70,60 100,75 130,55 160,18 190,72 220,66" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="40" cy="68" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="70" cy="60" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="100" cy="75" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="130" cy="55" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="160" cy="18" r="4" fill="#dc2626"/><circle cx="190" cy="72" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="220" cy="66" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/></svg>
figure.caption: Shewhart control chart

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a linear programming problem, the set of all points satisfying the constraints is the:
questions.solution: The feasible region is the (convex) set of all solutions meeting every constraint.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has a cycle time of 5 min and runs for 400 min/shift. The shift output is:
questions.solution: Output = available time / cycle time = 400/5 = 80 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The basic fundamental motions identified by the Gilbreths are called:
questions.solution: Therbligs (Gilbreth reversed) are 17–18 elemental hand motions used in micromotion study.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Safety stock is held primarily to protect against:
options: Uncertainty in demand and lead time
questions.solution: Safety (buffer) stock cushions against variability so stockouts are avoided.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the break-even chart shown (fixed cost ₹30000, price ₹100, variable cost ₹60/unit), the marked point where revenue meets total cost occurs at:
questions.solution: BEP = FC/(p − v) = 30000/(100 − 60) = 750 units, where total revenue = total cost.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 140" width="260" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="80" x2="240" y2="80" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="180" y="76" font-size="8" fill="#475569">total cost</text><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="220" y2="25" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="180" y="40" font-size="8" fill="#2563eb">revenue</text><circle cx="120" cy="62" r="4" fill="#dc2626"/><line x1="120" y1="62" x2="120" y2="120" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><text x="110" y="134" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">BEP</text></svg>
figure.caption: Break-even chart

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Johnson's rule is used to sequence n jobs through:
options: Any number of machines for profit
questions.solution: Johnson's rule gives the optimal job sequence on two machines minimizing total completion time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Daily demand = 80, lead time = 4 days, safety stock = 100 units. The reorder point is:
questions.solution: ROP = (demand × lead time) + safety stock = 80×4 + 100 = 420 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a product (line) layout, machines are arranged:
options: In the sequence of operations for the product
questions.solution: Product layout lines up resources in process order for high-volume, standardized output.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Queuing theory analyses systems characterised by:
questions.solution: Queuing models study arrival/service rates to predict waiting times and queue lengths.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has a cycle time of 2 min and runs for 420 min/shift. The shift output is:
questions.solution: Output = available time / cycle time = 420/2 = 210 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An X̄ (X-bar) control chart is used to monitor the process:
questions.solution: The X̄ chart tracks the central tendency (mean) of a process over time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A PERT activity has to = 2, tm = 8, tp = 12 days. Its expected time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Acceptance sampling is used to decide whether to accept or reject a:
questions.solution: A sample is inspected and the lot accepted/rejected per the sampling plan.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Method study (motion study) is primarily concerned with:
options: Finding the best way to do a job
Finding the time a job should take
questions.solution: Method study analyses and improves work methods; time study establishes the standard time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A task has a normal time of 4 min with 10% allowances. Its standard time is:
questions.solution: Standard time = normal × (1 + allowance) = 4×(1 + 10/100) = 4.4 min.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Aggregate planning sets production levels over a horizon of about:
options: Several months to a year (medium term)
questions.solution: Aggregate (medium-term) planning balances capacity and demand over months.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A PERT activity has to = 4 and tp = 11 days. Its standard deviation is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Using 3σ control limits, the probability of a point falling outside the limits when the process is in control is about:
questions.solution: About 99.73% lie within ±3σ, so ~0.27% fall outside by chance (false alarm).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The just-in-time (JIT) philosophy aims to:
options: Minimize inventory by producing only as needed
questions.solution: JIT pulls production to match demand, driving inventory (and waste) toward zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A line makes 600 units using 40 labour-hours. The labour productivity is:
questions.solution: Productivity = output/input = 600/40 = 15 units/hour.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linear programming is a technique to optimize a linear objective function subject to:
questions.solution: LP maximizes/minimizes a linear objective under a set of linear equality/inequality constraints.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the inventory cost curve shown (D = 2000, S = ₹50, H = ₹5), the order size Q* that minimizes total cost (where ordering and holding costs are equal) is:
questions.solution: Total cost is minimum at the EOQ Q* = √(2DS/H) = √(2×2000×50/5) ≈ 200 units, where the two cost curves cross.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 140" width="260" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M40 25 C 90 90 110 100 240 112" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="60" y="40" font-size="8" fill="#16a34a">ordering</text><line x1="35" y1="118" x2="235" y2="40" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="190" y="55" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">holding</text><path d="M40 60 C 110 30 120 30 235 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="120" y="28" font-size="8" fill="#2563eb">total cost</text><line x1="120" y1="48" x2="120" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><text x="110" y="134" font-size="8" fill="#475569">Q*</text></svg>
figure.caption: Inventory total-cost curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MRP (Material Requirements Planning) explodes the master schedule using the:
options: Bill of materials and inventory records
questions.solution: MRP nets requirements from the MPS, BOM and on-hand inventory to plan orders.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The reliability of a system of components in series is:
options: The product of the component reliabilities
questions.solution: Series system reliability Rs = R₁·R₂·…·Rn; it is lower than any single component.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the Pareto chart of defect causes shown (tallest bar = cause B), the principle being applied says you should first address:
options: Cause B (the tallest bar — the vital few)
questions.solution: The Pareto (80/20) principle prioritizes the 'vital few' tallest bars; cause B contributes the most defects and is tackled first.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 240 140" width="240" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="220" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="45" y="35" width="28" height="85" fill="#2563eb"/><text x="59" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">B</text><rect x="85" y="70" width="28" height="50" fill="#60a5fa"/><text x="99" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">P</text><rect x="125" y="92" width="28" height="28" fill="#93c5fd"/><text x="139" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">Q</text><rect x="165" y="105" width="28" height="15" fill="#bfdbfe"/><text x="179" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">R</text><polyline points="59,40 99,60 139,85 179,100" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>
figure.caption: Pareto chart of defects

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The PERT expected time of an activity (optimistic to, most-likely tm, pessimistic tp) is:
questions.solution: PERT uses a beta-distribution mean te = (to + 4tm + tp)/6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A product is used at 30 units/day with a lead time of 3 days. The reorder point (no safety stock) is:
questions.solution: ROP = demand rate × lead time = 30×3 = 90 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An R chart monitors the process:
questions.solution: The R chart tracks dispersion via the sample range, complementing the X̄ chart.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If the performance rating of a worker is above 100%, the normal time will be:
questions.solution: Normal = observed × rating; a rating >100% (faster than standard pace) raises normal time above observed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A task has a normal time of 8 min with 15% allowances. Its standard time is:
questions.solution: Standard time = normal × (1 + allowance) = 8×(1 + 15/100) = 9.2 min.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normal time is computed from the observed time using the:
questions.solution: Normal time = observed time × performance rating (rating normalizes worker pace).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A worker's observed time is 6 min at a performance rating of 110%. The normal time is:
questions.solution: Normal time = observed × rating = 6×110% = 6.6 min.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If annual demand quadruples, the EOQ (other things equal) changes by a factor of:
questions.solution: EOQ ∝ √D, so a 4× demand gives √4 = 2× the order quantity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) minimizes the sum of:
questions.solution: EOQ balances annual ordering cost against annual holding cost to minimize total inventory cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the inventory cost curve shown (D = 4000, S = ₹40, H = ₹5), the order size Q* that minimizes total cost (where ordering and holding costs are equal) is:
questions.solution: Total cost is minimum at the EOQ Q* = √(2DS/H) = √(2×4000×40/5) ≈ 253 units, where the two cost curves cross.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 140" width="260" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M40 25 C 90 90 110 100 240 112" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="60" y="40" font-size="8" fill="#16a34a">ordering</text><line x1="35" y1="118" x2="235" y2="40" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="190" y="55" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">holding</text><path d="M40 60 C 110 30 120 30 235 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="120" y="28" font-size="8" fill="#2563eb">total cost</text><line x1="120" y1="48" x2="120" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><text x="110" y="134" font-size="8" fill="#475569">Q*</text></svg>
figure.caption: Inventory total-cost curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In exponential smoothing, a larger smoothing constant α makes the forecast:
questions.solution: Higher α weights the latest actual more, tracking changes faster (but noisier).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost ₹20000, price ₹100, variable cost ₹70/unit. The output needed for a target profit of ₹10000 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Total Quality Management (TQM) emphasizes:
options: Quality as the QC department's sole job
questions.solution: TQM is an organization-wide, customer-focused, continuous-improvement culture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A weighted moving average differs from a simple moving average by:
options: Assigning different weights to different periods
questions.solution: Weighted MA typically gives more weight to recent periods, improving responsiveness.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the break-even chart shown (fixed cost ₹20000, price ₹80, variable cost ₹40/unit), the marked point where revenue meets total cost occurs at:
questions.solution: BEP = FC/(p − v) = 20000/(80 − 40) = 500 units, where total revenue = total cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Adding components in parallel (redundancy) generally:
questions.solution: Parallel redundancy means the system works if any one path works, raising reliability.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost = ₹50000, price = ₹100/unit, variable cost = ₹30/unit. The break-even quantity is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Reducing an activity's duration by adding resources, at extra cost, is called:
questions.solution: Crashing shortens critical activities at the least incremental cost to compress the schedule.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A simple moving-average forecast gives:
options: Weight only to the latest period
Equal weight to each period in the window
questions.solution: An n-period moving average averages the last n observations equally.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 50, UCL = 59, LCL = 41), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
In control with only chance variation
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 59) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Assembly-line balancing aims to:
options: Equalize workload across stations to minimize idle time
questions.solution: Line balancing assigns tasks so station times are nearly equal, reducing bottlenecks and idle time.

questions.stem: For D = 4900/yr, S = ₹50, H = ₹4/unit/yr, the minimum total ordering-plus-holding cost (at EOQ ≈ 350) is about:
